introduction: A chronic inflammatory disease of the airways that is characterized by hyper-responsiveness of the tracheo-bronchial tree to a multiplicity of stimuli. Manifests physiologically by wide-spread airway narrowing and clinically by paroxysmal attacks of dyspnoea, cough and wheezing. Acute episodes are interspersed with symptom-free periods.

features: Cough: unproductive, or productive of scanty sputum

investigations: Airway reversibility to inhaled β - adrenergic agonist
Isocapnoeic response to hyperventilation of cold air

goals: Prevent (or at least reduce) frequencies of asthmatic attacks
Maintain the best pulmonary function possible
drug: Nebulised salbutamol (Adult and child over 18 months): 2.5 mg repeated up to 3 times in the 1st hour, and subsequently 4 hourly for the next 24 hours; may be increased to 5 mg if necessary
Nebulised salbutamol (Child under 18 months): 1.25 - 2.5 mg same procedure as above
Intravenous aminophylline in patients with life threatening asthma not previously treated with theophylline and without contraindications. Adult: 125 - 250 mg slowly over 20 minutes
Intravenous aminophylline (Child 1 month - 18 years): by intravenous injection 5 mg/kg (maximum 500 mg), then by intravenous infusion
Oral or Intravenous steroids where indicated
Magnesium sulphate: 50 mg/kg single dose given slowly intravenously
Adequate hydration
Oxygen
Intermittent symptoms (Adult & Adolescents 12 years and older): Low dose Inhaled Corticosteroids/Formoterol or low dose Inhaled Corticosteroid (ICS)
Intermittent symptoms (Children 6 – 11 years): Low dose Inhaled Corticosteroid
Mild persistent asthma (Adult & Adolescents 12 years and older): Daily low dose ICS, or as-needed low dose ICS-formoterol
Mild persistent asthma (Children 6 – 11 years): Daily low dose ICS
Inhaled salbutamol (Adult): 100 - 200 μg (2 – 4 puffs)
Inhaled salbutamol (Child 1 month - 18 years): 100 - 200 μg (1 - 2 puffs)
Inhaled corticosteroids: Beclomethasone dipropionate 100 -200 μg 3 – 4 times daily
Inhaled corticosteroids: Budesonide 100 -200 μg every 12 hours
Inhaled corticosteroids: Fluticasone 50, 100, 125, 250 μg every 12 hours
Leukotriene receptor antagonist (LTRA)
Moderate persistent asthma (Adult & Adolescents 12 years and older): Low dose Inhaled Corticosteroid/Long acting beta agonist (ICS/LABA)
Moderate persistent asthma (Children 6 – 11 years): Low dose Inhaled Corticosteroid/Long acting beta agonist (ICS/LABA)
Severe persistent asthma (Adult & Adolescents 12 years and older): High dose ICS-LABA plus add on Tiotropium
Severe persistent asthma (Children 6 – 11 years): Refer for phenotypic assessment
Add on options: Anti-IgE or Anti-IL5/5R or Anti-IL4R
Other options: Oral corticosteroid: Prednisolone (Adult) 40 - 50 mg orally daily, Child: 1 - 2 mg/kg orally once daily
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Consult product literature to confirm the strengths of various aerosol preparations
Aminophylline: Do not exceed 500 mg in 24 hours because of the risk of cardiac arrhythmias
Aminophylline: Avoid in elderly or in patients with arrhythmias and hyperthyroidism
Aminophylline: Exercise caution in hypertensive patients
Aminophylline: May cause CNS stimulation with insomnia and convulsions
Care should be taken in withdrawing steroids
supportive_measures: Education on care and precipitating factors

prevention: Training of patients in the techniques of the proper use of aerosols/spacer devices is important
